label: Bitcoin as a unit of account and Gresham's law
brief:
Money serves three classical functions: medium of exchange, store of value, and unit of account. Bitcoin clearly serves the first two for a meaningful and growing population of users — billions of dollars of value stored, millions of transactions per day. The unit-of-account question is more contested. Most prices in the world are still quoted in fiat, and even bitcoin-denominated services typically settle to a fiat reference at the moment of transaction.

The Austrian explanation for this lag has two parts. The first is *path dependence*: a unit of account is a coordination equilibrium, and switching costs are real. Sticky prices, accounting systems, tax codes, and customer expectations all anchor on whichever unit is currently dominant. Once enough of the economy denominates in dollars, an individual merchant who switches to bitcoin pricing imposes friction on their counterparties [1]. The second is *Gresham's law*: 'bad money drives out good' when both circulate as legal tender. Citizens hold the harder money and spend the softer one, because the soft money is what they want to be rid of and the hard money is what they want to accumulate [2]. This is exactly what we observe — bitcoin held, dollars spent.

Gresham's law is sometimes invoked as evidence that bitcoin will *never* become a medium of exchange and therefore never a unit of account. The Austrian counter is that Gresham's law assumes legal-tender parity between two monies — once that legal coercion lifts (or once one money's purchasing power degrades enough), the equilibrium flips and the harder money begins to dominate transaction flow. This is the historical pattern in every major hyperinflation: people abandoned the local fiat for a harder substitute as soon as the inflation became unbearable [3].

The order in which Bitcoin acquires its three monetary functions — store of value first, medium of exchange next, unit of account last — is therefore not a defect of Bitcoin's design but a *prediction* of standard monetary theory. The 'medium of exchange first' camp (associated with the Bitcoin Cash schism) argued the opposite ordering on engineering grounds; the 'store of value first' camp won out because the underlying economics pointed that way regardless of the engineering preferences.

sources.label: Ludwig von Mises — The Theory of Money and Credit
sources.excerpt: The unit-of-account function of money is the slowest to migrate, because every contract, accounting standard, and habit anchors on the existing unit. Migration happens only when the existing unit visibly fails.

sources.label: Murray N. Rothbard — The Mystery of Banking
sources.excerpt: Gresham's law — bad money drives out good — is not a quirk but a logical consequence of legal-tender pricing. Citizens spend what they want to be rid of and hoard what they want to keep.

sources.label: Daniel Krawisz — Hyperbitcoinization
sources.excerpt: The transition is not gradual in its later stages. Once the harder money becomes the dominant store of value and a critical mass of transactions reference it directly, the unit-of-account function flips comparatively quickly.
